section.title: The LORD Promises to Bless Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: The answer to the fasting question turns from rebuke to sweeping promise. Ten times God says This is what the LORD of Hosts says, painting a restored Jerusalem full of old people and playing children, a faithful and righteous God dwelling among His people, fasts turned to feasts, and the nations streaming in to seek the LORD because God is with His people.
section.thesis: The LORD is zealously committed to bless Jerusalem: He will return to dwell in her, make her the City of Truth, turn her mourning fasts into joyful feasts, and draw the nations to seek Him, all grounded in His own faithful and righteous character, and calling His people to lives of truth, justice, and peace.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God's jealous return to dwell in a restored Jerusalem of old and young; His pledge to save and gather His people as their faithful God, with the call to strong hands and truthful lives; the turning of fasts into feasts; and the nations streaming in because God is with His people.

units.label: God returns to dwell in Zion

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD of Hosts came to me, saying:
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “I am {{qana|jealous}} for Zion with great zeal; I am jealous for her with great fervor.”

This is what the LORD says: “I will return to Zion and dwell in Jerusalem. Then Jerusalem will be called the City of {{emet|Truth}}, and the mountain of the LORD of Hosts will be called the Holy Mountain.”
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Old men and old women will again sit along the streets of Jerusalem, each with a staff in hand because of great age.
And the streets of the city will be filled with boys and girls playing there.”
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “If this is impossible in the eyes of the remnant of this people in these days, should it also be impossible in My eyes?” declares the LORD of Hosts.

units.label: I will save and gather My people

verses.text: This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “I will save My people from the land of the east and from the land of the west.
I will bring them back to dwell in Jerusalem, where they will be My people, and I will be their faithful and righteous God.”

This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Let your hands be strong, you who now hear these words spoken by the prophets who were present when the foundations were laid to rebuild the temple, the house of the LORD of Hosts.
For before those days neither man nor beast received wages, nor was there safety from the enemy for anyone who came or went, for I had turned every man against his neighbor.
But now I will not treat the remnant of this people as I did in the past,” declares the LORD of Hosts.

“For the seed will be prosperous, the vine will yield its fruit, the ground will yield its produce, and the skies will give their dew. To the remnant of this people I will give all these things as an inheritance.
As you have been a curse among the nations, O house of Judah and house of Israel, so I will save you, and you will be a blessing. Do not be afraid; let your hands be strong.”
For this is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Just as I resolved to bring disaster upon you when your fathers provoked Me to anger, and I did not relent,” says the LORD of Hosts,
“so now I have resolved to do good again to Jerusalem and Judah. Do not be afraid.
These are the things you must do: Speak truth to one another, render true and sound judgments in your gates,
do not plot evil in your hearts against your neighbor, and do not love to swear falsely, for I hate all these things,” declares the LORD.
Then the word of the LORD of Hosts came to me, saying,

“This is what the LORD of Hosts says: The fasts of the fourth, the fifth, the seventh, and the tenth months will become times of joy and gladness, cheerful feasts for the house of Judah. Therefore you are to love both truth and {{shalom|peace}}.”

units.label: The nations stream to seek the LORD

verses.text: This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Peoples will yet come, the residents of many cities,
and the residents of one city will go to another, saying: ‘Let us go at once to plead before the LORD and to seek the LORD of Hosts. I myself am going.’
And many peoples and strong nations will come to seek the LORD of Hosts in Jerusalem and to plead before the LORD.”

This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “In those days ten men from the nations of every tongue will tightly grasp the robe of a Jew, saying, ‘Let us go with you, for we have heard that God is with you.’”

qana.gloss: Jealous, zealous, burning with protective and possessive love. God's jealousy for Zion is not petty envy but the fierce, faithful devotion of a husband for his bride; it is the same zeal that will not share His people with idols and that drives Him to save, restore, and dwell with them. His jealousy is the engine of Jerusalem's blessing.

emet.gloss: Truth, faithfulness, reliability, that which is firm and trustworthy. When Jerusalem is called the City of Truth, it means a community marked by God's own faithfulness, where words can be trusted, judgments are sound, and no one deals falsely. It flows from God, the faithful and righteous God of verse eight, into the character He calls His people to live.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being, flourishing in every relationship, with God, neighbor, and creation. Far more than the absence of conflict, shalom is the rich, restored life God promises to Jerusalem, seen in old people at rest and children at play. God's people are to love both truth and peace, the twin marks of the city God blesses.

basic.context: This is the joyful other half of the answer to the fasting question. Ten times God declares, This is what the LORD of Hosts says, piling up promises. He is jealous for Zion and will return to dwell there. Jerusalem, once desolate, will be the City of Truth, full of old people sitting safely and children playing in the streets. He will save and gather His scattered people, turn their curse into blessing, transform their mourning fasts into glad feasts, and draw many nations to seek Him because God is with His people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic promise, rich in imagery of restored ordinary life. The pictures of old age and children at play signal peace and security. The promises are grounded in God's character and reach beyond the immediate return from exile to the wider ingathering of the nations, fulfilled as the gospel goes out. The call to truth, justice, and peace shows that grace produces a changed community.
basic.originalLanguages: God's driving motive is that He is qana, jealous, fiercely and faithfully devoted to Zion. He makes her the City of emet, truth and faithfulness, reflecting His own reliability. And He calls His people to love both truth and shalom, peace, the wholeness and flourishing that mark the city He blesses.
basic.typology: God's return to dwell in Zion points to Christ, Immanuel, God with us, and to the church as God's temple by the Spirit. The nations streaming to seek the LORD in Jerusalem is fulfilled as the gospel gathers people of every tongue into the true Israel. The turning of fasts into feasts anticipates the joy of the kingdom.
basic.christ: God's promise to return and dwell with His people is fulfilled supremely in Jesus, Immanuel, God with us in the flesh, and then in the Spirit who makes His people God's dwelling. The word that drew ten men from the nations to grasp a Jew's robe because God is with you is fulfilled as the nations come to Christ, the true Israelite, in whom God is with us for salvation.
basic.god: God is jealous with a saving, faithful love, resolved to do good to His people as firmly as He once resolved to judge. He is the faithful and righteous God who keeps covenant, reverses curses into blessing, and turns mourning into joy. He is a missionary God who intends His blessing of one people to draw all peoples to Himself.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 2 and Micah 4 foresee the nations streaming to the mountain of the LORD. Matthew 1 names Jesus Immanuel, God with us. Revelation 21 shows God dwelling with His people. Zechariah 8:23 is fulfilled as people of every tongue seek the God of the Jews in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The rebuke of chapter 7 gives way to sweeping promise: God will return to dwell in Zion, bless His people, turn fasts to feasts, and draw the nations. This runs to Christ, Immanuel, in whom God dwells with us and gathers people of every tongue, and on to the new Jerusalem where God dwells with His people forever.

context.literary: The second half of the answer to the delegation's fasting question, matching the rebuke of chapter 7 with a flood of promise. The repeated formula, This is what the LORD of Hosts says, ten times, structures the chapter into a cascade of assurances that climax in the nations coming to seek the LORD, forming a bridge to the two burdens of chapters 9 to 14.
context.historical: The returned community was small, poor, and discouraged, its temple half-built and its city still shaken by the memory of the recent hard years of no wages and no safety. Into that discouragement God speaks pictures of ordinary flourishing, safe old age and children playing, to fire their hope and strengthen their hands for the work of rebuilding.
context.canonical: The vision of the nations streaming to Jerusalem to seek the LORD joins Isaiah 2, Micah 4, and Zechariah's own later chapters, and is fulfilled in the New Testament as the gospel gathers the nations. God's return to dwell in Zion runs from the tabernacle and temple through the incarnation to the Spirit-indwelt church and the new Jerusalem.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the fasting question with overwhelming assurance of God's commitment to bless Jerusalem, to strengthen the discouraged builders, to turn their mourning into joy, and to call them to truth, justice, and peace as the fitting life of the people God is gathering, including the nations.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The promises of restoration and ingathering are God's declared purpose; the commands to speak truth, judge justly, and love peace are prescriptive for God's people. The abiding truths are God's faithful zeal to bless, His ingathering of the nations, and the changed life grace produces.

debates.question: Do these promises refer to the return from exile or to a wider fulfillment?

views.label: Fulfilled progressively, beginning with the return
views.case: The immediate horizon is the restored post-exilic community, but the language of the nations seeking the LORD reaches beyond it to the gospel age and the new creation.

views.label: Ultimately fulfilled in Christ and the church
views.case: The full sweep, God dwelling with His people and every tongue seeking Him, is realized in Christ, Immanuel, and the ingathering of the nations, consummated in the new Jerusalem.

debates.bearing: Either way the promises are certain and expanding, moving from the return through Christ to the final dwelling of God with a people from every nation.

debates.question: What does grasping the robe of a Jew signify?

views.label: Gentiles seeking the God of Israel
views.case: People of every tongue attach themselves to the Jew because God is with him, picturing the nations coming to worship the true God through Israel.

views.label: The nations coming through the true Israelite, Christ
views.case: The one with whom God is present supremely is Jesus, the true Israel, so the image points to the nations laying hold of salvation in Him.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the ingathering of the nations to the God who is with His people, fulfilled as the gospel goes to every tongue in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Three words open the heart of the chapter. Behind every promise is that God is qana, jealous for Zion, a word of fierce, faithful, protective love, the passion of a husband for his bride, the same zeal that refuses to share His people with idols and drives Him to save and dwell with them. The result is that Jerusalem becomes the City of emet, truth and faithfulness, a community that mirrors God's own reliability, where words can be trusted and judgments are sound. And the life He calls His people to is summed up as loving both truth and shalom, peace, the deep wholeness and flourishing pictured in old people sitting safely in the streets and children at play. Jealous love, truthfulness, and peace, these are the marks of the city God is committed to bless.

typology.type: God returning to dwell in Zion
typology.fulfillment: Christ, Immanuel, God with us, and the Spirit indwelling the church as God's temple, Matthew 1:23 and Ephesians 2:22.

typology.type: The nations streaming to seek the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The gospel gathering people of every tongue to Christ, Isaiah 2:2-3 and Revelation 7:9.
The joy of the kingdom and the marriage supper of the Lamb, Isaiah 25:6-9 and Revelation 19:9.

study.christ: The engine of this chapter is God's promise to return and dwell with His people, and that promise finds its face in Jesus. He is Immanuel, God with us, the Word made flesh who tabernacled among us, God come at last to dwell in the midst of His people not in symbol but in person. Through His death and resurrection and the gift of the Spirit, His people themselves become the City of Truth, the temple where God dwells. And the closing scene, ten men of every tongue seizing the robe of a Jew because they have heard that God is with you, is fulfilled as the nations lay hold of salvation in the true Israelite, Christ, in whom God is decisively with us for our rescue. The zealous, jealous love that would not let Zion go is the same love that gave the Son, so that a people from every nation might dwell forever with their faithful and righteous God.
study.god: This chapter overflows with the character of God. He is jealous for His people with a fierce and faithful love, and that love is the reason for every promise. He is resolved, as firmly committed to do good now as He once was to bring judgment, so that His people can build with strong hands and without fear. He is the faithful and righteous God who keeps covenant, who reverses their curse into blessing and their mourning fasts into feasts, and who cares that the community He blesses lives in truth, justice, and peace. And He is a missionary God, intending the blessing of Zion to overflow to the nations, drawing many peoples and strong nations to seek Him, because His purpose was never one people only but a world of worshippers gathered to Himself.

crossReferences.note: Jesus is called Immanuel, God with us, fulfilling God's promise to return and dwell in the midst of His people.
The nations stream to the mountain of the LORD to be taught His ways, matching the nations seeking the LORD here.
God's dwelling is with man, and He will dwell with them, the consummation of I will return to Zion.
Ten men of every tongue grasp a Jew's robe because God is with you, fulfilled as the nations come to Christ.

study.oneStory: The rebuke of chapter 7 opens into a cascade of promise: God will return to dwell in Zion, bless His people, turn fasts into feasts, and draw the nations to seek Him. This runs straight to Christ, Immanuel, in whom God dwells with us and gathers people of every tongue, and on to the new Jerusalem where God dwells with His people, faithful and righteous, forever.

quiz.q: What is God's driving motive for blessing Jerusalem?

options.text: His jealous, faithful love, His zeal for Zion.
options.note: Verse 2, I am jealous for Zion with great zeal.

options.text: The people's impressive obedience.
options.note: The motive is God's own zeal, not the people's merit.

options.text: Pressure from the surrounding nations.
options.note: God acts from His own jealous love, not external pressure.

options.text: No stated reason.
options.note: God clearly names His jealous zeal as the reason.

quiz.q: What everyday pictures show Jerusalem's restored peace?

options.text: Old people sitting safely in the streets and children playing there.
options.note: Verses 4 and 5 picture safe old age and children at play.

options.text: Soldiers marching in formation.
options.note: The images are of peace and safety, not military might.

options.text: Merchants growing rich on trade.
options.note: The picture is peaceful ordinary life, not wealth.

options.text: Empty, silent streets.
options.note: The streets are full of the old and the young, not empty.

quiz.q: What will happen to the people's mourning fasts?

options.text: They will become times of joy and gladness, cheerful feasts.
options.note: Verse 19 turns the fasts into feasts.

options.text: They will be kept forever unchanged.
options.note: God transforms them from mourning into joy.

options.text: They will be replaced by heavier fasting.
options.note: They become feasts, not heavier fasts.

options.text: They will be forgotten with no replacement.
options.note: They become joyful feasts, not simply abolished.

quiz.q: What does God call His people to love?

options.text: Both truth and peace.
options.note: Verse 19, therefore you are to love both truth and peace.

options.text: Only ritual observance.
options.note: He calls for truth and peace, not mere ritual.

options.text: Wealth and security above all.
options.note: The call is to truth and peace, not to riches.

options.text: Nothing in particular.
options.note: He specifically names truth and peace.

quiz.q: How is the promise of the nations seeking the LORD fulfilled?

options.text: The gospel gathers people of every tongue to Christ, in whom God is with us.
options.note: The nations grasp the robe of a Jew because God is with you, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: Only Israel is ever gathered, never the nations.
options.note: The text explicitly includes many peoples and strong nations.

options.text: The nations come only to make war.
options.note: They come to seek and worship the LORD, not to fight.

options.text: It is never fulfilled in any way.
options.note: It is fulfilled as the nations come to Christ.

questions: God is jealous for His people with a fierce, faithful love. How does it change your view of God to see His jealousy as the engine of your blessing rather than a threat?
God pictured blessing as safe old age and children playing. What would ordinary, God-given flourishing look like in your home and community?
God turned mourning fasts into feasts. Where is God inviting you to trade a season of grief for the joy He promises?
God calls His people to love both truth and peace. Which of the two do you find harder to pursue, honest truth or costly peace, and why?
The nations came because they heard God is with His people. What would others conclude about God from watching your life this week?

translationNotes.note: Jealous renders qanna, the fierce, faithful, protective love of God for His people, not petty envy; the doubled phrasing intensifies His zeal for Zion.
Impossible can also be rendered marvelous, appearing twice in the verse; what seems impossible to the small remnant is not impossible or too marvelous for God.
Ten men from the nations of every tongue grasping the robe of a Jew pictures the ingathering of the nations to the God who is with His people, fulfilled as the gospel goes to every tongue.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 8:1-23. All 23 verses reproduced in order. One source em dash in verse 20 (Peoples will yet come, the residents of many cities) rendered as commas per style.
